questions.q: What is the primary purpose of Lamport timestamps in distributed systems?
options: To provide a total ordering of events across processes by assigning monotonically increasing integers
To synchronize physical clocks so all nodes show the exact same time
To measure the latency between nodes in a distributed network
To detect and correct Byzantine failures in message ordering
questions.explanation: Lamport timestamps assign logical values to events to create a causal ordering, not requiring clock synchronization. Option 1 is about physical clock synchronization, which Lamport timestamps avoid. Option 2 measures network delay, unrelated to logical ordering.

questions.q: How does the Lamport timestamp algorithm ensure causality?
options: Each process increments its timestamp for every local event and updates it when receiving messages with higher timestamps
By synchronizing all clocks to a central authority at regular intervals
By requiring that all messages are delivered in the exact order they were sent
Through a global timestamp service that assigns values to all events
questions.explanation: Lamport's rule: increment on local events and set timestamp to max(local, received) + 1 on message receipt. Option 1 requires clock synchronization. Option 2 assumes perfect FIFO delivery. Option 3 would create a bottleneck and single point of failure.

questions.q: What limitation does a Lamport clock have regarding causality?
options: It can create a total order of events that respects causality, but two causally unrelated events may have artificially ordered timestamps
It requires all nodes to have synchronized physical clocks
It cannot detect when events occur simultaneously
It fails entirely if any single node's clock becomes unreliable
questions.explanation: Lamport clocks enforce a total order but lose information about which events are truly independent. Option 1 is the definition of Lamport's approach. Option 2 confuses Lamport with physical synchronization. Option 3 is incorrect; simultaneous events are ordered arbitrarily.

questions.q: How many components does a vector clock have in a system with n processes?
options: Exactly n integers, one per process
A single integer that grows proportionally to n
One timestamp per pair of processes, totaling n(n-1)/2 values
A variable number depending on the number of messages exchanged
questions.explanation: Vector clocks maintain an array of n counters, allowing full causality tracking. Option 1 is a misconception that one global counter suffices. Option 2 is incorrect; vector clocks don't scale with message count.

questions.q: What distinguishes the 'happens-before' relationship from physical causality?
options: Happens-before is a logical construct that may include events with no actual causal connection, defined by message passing and local sequencing
Happens-before precisely corresponds to causality observed in the real world with no false positives
Happens-before only accounts for message-passing events and ignores local computation
Happens-before requires that all events are ordered by wall-clock time
questions.explanation: Happens-before formalizes message passing and local sequencing but may order unrelated events; it is a partial order, not total causality. Option 1 assumes happens-before matches physical causality perfectly. Option 2 ignores local events. Option 3 contradicts the use of logical clocks.

questions.q: In a vector clock system, when can you conclude that event A causally precedes event B?
options: When A's vector clock is less than or equal to B's in all components and strictly less in at least one
When A's timestamp is less than B's timestamp in any single component
When A and B both occurred on processes that exchanged a message
When A occurred before B in wall-clock time across any two nodes
questions.explanation: Vector clock dominance (≤ in all, < in at least one) precisely captures causal precedence. Option 1 uses incomplete comparison. Option 2 doesn't require dominance. Option 3 assumes message exchange implies precedence direction.

questions.q: What is the key difference between vector clocks and Lamport clocks in detecting concurrency?
options: Vector clocks can definitively identify causally unrelated (concurrent) events; Lamport clocks cannot
Lamport clocks are more precise and require less storage than vector clocks
Vector clocks only work in systems with exactly 3 processes
Lamport clocks detect concurrency by comparing integer differences
questions.explanation: Vector clocks allow determination of partial order; two events are concurrent if neither dominates the other. Lamport clocks create a total order, obscuring concurrency. Option 1 is false; Lamport clocks are more storage-efficient. Option 2 is baseless; Lamport clocks don't measure concurrency.

questions.q: How does a process update its vector clock when sending a message?
options: It increments its own position, then sends the updated vector to the recipient
It sends only the incremented value without the entire vector
It waits for all other processes to acknowledge before incrementing
It replaces its value with the maximum seen across all processes
questions.explanation: Correct protocol: increment self, then send the vector. This records the send event in the sender's timestamp. Option 1 omits the vector information needed at the receiver. Option 2 creates waiting dependencies. Option 3 violates the increment-on-send rule.

questions.q: What problem arises from using only Lamport timestamps to order all events in a system with millions of concurrent processes?
options: Events are ordered but the ordering may be arbitrary for causally unrelated events, making debugging and replication harder
Lamport timestamps cannot function with more than 1000 processes
All clocks will eventually overflow and wrap around to zero
Messages will arrive out of order due to network delays
questions.explanation: Lamport clocks scale well numerically but lose causality information—unrelated events get artificial order. Option 1 is a false limit. Option 2 is unrelated to causality issues. Option 3 assumes perfect timestamp propagation is the goal.

questions.q: In the context of the causality principle in distributed systems, what is the significance of the partial order relationship?
options: It allows some events to remain unordered (concurrent), preserving the structure of true causal dependencies while avoiding artificial orderings
It guarantees that every pair of events has a defined causal relationship
It ensures that all messages arrive in the order they were sent
It requires processes to block until causally dependent events complete
questions.explanation: Partial order respects causality while allowing concurrent events to remain incomparable, unlike total orders. Option 1 assumes all events must be comparable. Option 2 is inconsistent with concurrent event handling. Option 3 contradicts non-blocking systems.
